pattern: Because a move can retarget any element to any value, the optimal use of at most three moves is always to erase some number of extreme values from one or both sorted ends, so only four sorted-array windows need comparing.
hints: Changing an element to any value is at least as powerful as deleting it from consideration for the min/max, so think of the three moves as 'remove up to three elements, then look at the range of what's left'.
Sort the array first. The elements you'd remove are always some of the smallest and some of the largest - never one from the middle.
With the array sorted, try all four ways to split three removals between the two ends (0+3, 1+2, 2+1, 3+0) and take the smallest resulting max-minus-min window.

edge_inputs.description: four elements collapse to a single point
five elements, one outlier on each side
three elements, always reducible to zero difference
length at most four is always answerable with difference zero
single element needs no moves at all
one extreme outlier on each side of a length-5 array requires checking every 0..3 split, not just the two ends
extreme values at both ends of the allowed range

stress.description: small arrays that exercise the independent combinations-based brute force oracle
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(5, min(size, 9))
    nums = [rng.randint(-1000000000, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

stress.description: large random arrays near the maximum length
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(5, size)
    nums = [rng.randint(-1000000000, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
